# LeanFpAnalysis Library Lookup

This guide is a public map of the floating-point analysis library. It is meant
for Lean users, documentation tools, and automated agents that need to discover
which files and theorem names are relevant to a stability-analysis goal.

It is not a proof-script guide: it maps library concepts and theorem names
without giving task-specific scripts.

## How To Import

For exploratory work, start with:

```lean
import LeanFpAnalysis.FP
open LeanFpAnalysis.FP
```

For a smaller import, use the file listed in the tables below.

For the completed deterministic RandNLA Algorithm 1 stability proof ledger, see
[`RANDNLA_ALGORITHM1_STABILITY_LEDGER.md`](RANDNLA_ALGORITHM1_STABILITY_LEDGER.md).

For the theorem-style prose summary of the Algorithm 2 row-sampling results,
see [`Algorithm2_RowSampling_Stability_Proof_Summary.pdf`](Algorithm2_RowSampling_Stability_Proof_Summary.pdf).

For the theorem-style paper-level summary of the RandNLA CACM formalization,
see [`RandNLA_CACM_Formalization_Summary.pdf`](RandNLA_CACM_Formalization_Summary.pdf).

For the live theorem ledger of the full-paper extraction, classifications,
Lean theorem names, hypotheses, random variables, events, and next proof steps,
see [`RANDNLA_CACM_THEOREM_LEDGER.md`](RANDNLA_CACM_THEOREM_LEDGER.md).

For the open RandNLA accuracy backlog, the full-paper algorithm/application
inventory, and the deterministic subtheorems already closed, see
[`RANDNLA_CACM_NOT_PROVED_LEDGER.md`](RANDNLA_CACM_NOT_PROVED_LEDGER.md).
That ledger is the authoritative full-paper gate: open paper-level rows mean
the full CACM formalization request is still incomplete, even when all current
Lean modules build. In autonomous runs, those open rows are the continuation
queue: after recording the failed gate, pick the highest-leverage open row and
continue with the next concrete Lean foundation rather than stopping at the
diagnosis.

For the front-loaded proof-source acquisition pass, source-chain decisions,
and exact external theorem/lemma/equation references for open paper-level
items, see
[`RANDNLA_CACM_PROOF_SOURCE_LEDGER.md`](RANDNLA_CACM_PROOF_SOURCE_LEDGER.md).
That file is the guide for avoiding repeated adjacent lemmas around a
bottleneck: when an uploaded proof is incomplete, record the primary source
chain and chosen proof route there before adding new Lean infrastructure.

For the page-by-page audit of the local Higham Chapter 1 excerpt, see
[`CHAPTER01_FORMALIZATION_LEDGER.md`](CHAPTER01_FORMALIZATION_LEDGER.md).
The local `references/Chapter01.pdf` has six PDF pages and ends at the opening
of §1.3; the ledger records the exact excerpt scope and the Lean names closing
its formalizable definitions and results.

For the audit of the full local Chapter 1 PDF, see
[`CHAPTER01_FULL_FORMALIZATION_LEDGER.md`](CHAPTER01_FULL_FORMALIZATION_LEDGER.md).
That ledger is the authoritative gate for `references/Chapter01_full.pdf`:
open paper-level rows mean the full Chapter 1 formalization is still
incomplete, even when the current Lean modules build.
For Chapter 1 proof-source acquisition and route decisions for open
paper-level rows, see
[`CHAPTER01_PROOF_SOURCE_LEDGER.md`](CHAPTER01_PROOF_SOURCE_LEDGER.md).

For the line-by-line and result-by-result audit of the local Higham Chapter 2
PDF, see [`CHAPTER02_FORMALIZATION_LEDGER.md`](CHAPTER02_FORMALIZATION_LEDGER.md).
That ledger is the authoritative gate for `References/Chapter02_full.pdf`:
the abstract standard model (2.4), inverse-model algebra for (2.5), no-guard
model surface for (2.6), and normalized finite-format range/adjacency layer are
partly closed, but full IEEE semantics, guard-digit exact-subtraction theorems,
probabilistic/statistical claims, and Chapter 2 problems remain open.

For the line-by-line and result-by-result audit of the local Higham Chapter 3
PDF, see [`CHAPTER03_FORMALIZATION_LEDGER.md`](CHAPTER03_FORMALIZATION_LEDGER.md).
That ledger is the authoritative gate for `References/Chapter03.pdf`: dot
products, signed products, outer products, matrix-vector products,
matrix-matrix products, rounded complex scalar arithmetic, and rank-one update
are partially or fully closed as recorded there. No-guard/block and
extended-precision dot-product variants, saxpy loop-order equivalence, and a
few model/notation adapters remain open.

## Exact Algebra And Norm Policy

Mathlib is the source of truth for exact dot products, matrix algebra, and
norms.  Use Mathlib notation directly when the object already has a
Mathlib-native type, for example `x ⬝ᵥ y`, `‖WithLp.toLp 2 x‖`, or `‖A‖` for
`A : RMat m n` under the appropriate matrix norm scope.

Some existing algorithm APIs still use function-shaped matrices
`RMatFn m n := Fin m → Fin n → ℝ`.  For those legacy APIs, use compatibility
wrappers such as `frobNorm` and `infNorm`.  These wrappers are bridges to
Mathlib norms via `Matrix.of`; they are not independent norm definitions.

New exact matrix-facing APIs should prefer `RMat m n := Matrix (Fin m) (Fin n)
ℝ`, especially for rectangular algorithms such as QR and least squares.
Existing `fl_*` algorithms may continue using `RMatFn` while the implementation
layer is migrated gradually.

## Goal-To-Theorem Lookup

| Higham Chapter 1 reverse inverse-square high-prefix standard-model adapter | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/InstabilityWithoutCancellation.lean` | `inverseSquareSingleReverseAccumulatorFrom`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixState`, `inverseSquareTerm`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteNormalRange`, `FloatingPointFormat.ieeeSingleFormat.unitRoundoff`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixStdErrorEnvelope` | `inverseSquareTerm_ten_pow_nine_le_of_pos_le`, `inverseSquareTerm_ten_pow_nine_ge_ieeeSingle_minNormal`, `inverseSquareTerm_ge_ieeeSingle_minNormal_of_pos_le_ten_pow_nine`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseAccumulatorFrom_le_of_le_steps`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixState_prefix_le_highPrefix`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixStep_exactInput_finiteNormalRange`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixStep_standardModel_lt`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixStep_abs_error_lt_unitRoundoff_mul_exactInput`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixStep_abs_error_lt_unitRoundoff_mul_coarse`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixStepStdError`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixStdErrorEnvelope`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixStepStdError_nonneg`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixStdErrorEnvelope_nonneg`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefix_abs_error_le_stdErrorEnvelope`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixState_abs_error_le_stdErrorEnvelope` | Gives a single quantified bridge over all high-index reverse-prefix additions in §1.12.3. Lean proves the term is normal-sized, every earlier rounded prefix state is bounded by the final high-prefix state, the exact addition input is binary32 finite-normal for every `j < 10^9 - 4096`, and each such rounded addition has a standard relative-error factor with `|delta| < unitRoundoff`. It also converts that factor into `|fl(s+t)-(s+t)| < u*(s+t)`, the uniform coarse high-prefix bound `u*(1/2048 + 2^-24)`, and a recursive accumulated envelope proving the final high-prefix state differs from the exact high-prefix mass by at most the sum of those local envelopes. This is a whole-prefix adapter, not thousands of individual step proofs. |
| Higham Chapter 1 reverse inverse-square high-prefix `8192` split foothold | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/InstabilityWithoutCancellation.lean` | `inverseSquareTerm`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseAccumulatorFrom`, `inverseSquareExactReverseAccumulatorFrom`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixState` | `inverseSquareTerm_le_two_pow_neg_26_of_ge_8192`, `inverseSquareReverseTenPowNineHighPrefix_index_ge_8192`, `inverseSquareTerm_le_two_pow_neg_26_of_reverse_ten_pow_nine_high_binade`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixState_split_8192`, `inverseSquareExactReverseTenPowNineHighPrefix_split_8192`, `inverseSquareExactReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixBefore8192_le_inv_8192`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixBefore8192_le_inv_4096`, `inverseSquareExactReverseBinade8192To4097_le_inv_8192`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseBinade8192To4097_le_start_add_inv_4096`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseHighPrefixCandidateWindowCellGuardMarginShiftedLowerBound_lt_inv_4096` | Closes the first binade-scale dependency for the remaining D1 high-prefix proof. Lean proves all terms with `k >= 8192` are at most `2^-26`, specializes that bound to the earlier `10^9, ..., 8193` high-prefix block, and splits both the rounded and exact high-prefix states into `10^9, ..., 8193` followed by `8192, ..., 4097`. It also proves the exact earlier-block mass is at most `1/8192`, the rounded earlier-block state is at most `1/4096`, the exact final-binade mass is at most `1/8192`, and a rounded `8192, ..., 4097` binade begun from any finite binary32 start increases by at most `1/4096`. The guard `inverseSquareSingleReverseHighPrefixCandidateWindowCellGuardMarginShiftedLowerBound_lt_inv_4096` records that this coarse increment budget is still larger than the strict finite-cell D1 margin, so these are structural high-prefix block data, not a closing candidate-window proof. |
| Higham Chapter 1 reverse inverse-square exact earlier-block start window | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/InstabilityWithoutCancellation.lean` | `inverseSquareExactReverseAccumulatorFrom`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseHighPrefixBefore8192WindowLower`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseHighPrefixBefore8192WindowUpper` | `inverseSquareSingleReverseHighPrefixBefore8192WindowLower`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseHighPrefixBefore8192WindowUpper`, `inverseSquareExactReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixBefore8192_mem_startWindow` | Closes the exact side of the `8192` split start-window dependency. Lean defines the binary32 lower and upper endpoints for the earlier `10^9, ..., 8193` block feeding the final high-prefix binade, then proves by telescoping that the exact earlier-block mass lies inside this start window. This is not the rounded D1 proof; the next high-prefix dependency is to place the rounded earlier-block state in the same window and compose it with a compact `8192, ..., 4097` binade window map. |

After the two boundary additions `8192^{-2}` and `8191^{-2}`, the terms `8190^{-2}, ..., 4097^{-2}` stay in binary32 exponent `-12` and use scale `q = 37`; Lean proves the scaled mantissa prefix total is `8385036` and verifies the endpoint-safety predicate for all 4094 tail steps by a single kernel-checked Boolean certificate. This certificate is now consumed by the final high-binade window-map row below; the remaining D1 high-prefix work is rounded earlier-block membership in the pre-`8192` start window. |
| Higham Chapter 1 reverse inverse-square final high-binade window map | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/InstabilityWithoutCancellation.lean` | `inverseSquareSingleReverseAccumulatorFrom`, `inverseSquareSingleForwardStep`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseHighPrefixBefore8192WindowLower`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseHighPrefixBefore8192WindowUpper`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseHighPrefixCandidateWindowLower`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseHighPrefixCandidateWindowUpper` | `inverseSquareSingleReverseHighBinadeAfter8192WindowLower`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseHighBinadeAfter8192WindowUpper`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseHighBinadeBefore8192Window_round_8192_step_mem_after8192Window`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseHighBinadeAfter8191WindowLower`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseHighBinadeAfter8191WindowUpper`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseHighBinadeAfter8192Window_round_8191_step_mem_after8191Window`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseHighBinadeTailWindow_round_step_mem`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseHighBinadeTailWindow_prefix_mem`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseHighBinade8190To4097Window_closed`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseHighBinade8192To4097WindowMapsToCandidate_closed`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixBefore8192InStartWindow`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixInCandidateWindow_of_before8192StartWindow` | Closes the compact final-binade window map for `8192^{-2}, ..., 4097^{-2}`. Lean proves the `8192^{-2}` boundary maps the pre-binade start window into the post-`8192` window, the `8191^{-2}` boundary maps into the same-exponent tail window by adjacent-cell nearest rounding, and the 4094-step checked tail certificate propagates every start in that tail window into the high-prefix candidate window. This reduces the remaining D1 high-prefix obligation to the single proposition `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixBefore8192InStartWindow`, namely rounded earlier-block membership for `10^9, ..., 8193`; it does not close that rounded earlier-block obligation. |
| Higham Chapter 1 reverse inverse-square rounded earlier-block start-window bottleneck | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/InstabilityWithoutCancellation.lean` | `inverseSquareSingleReverseAccumulatorFrom`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseHighPrefixBefore8192WindowLower`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseHighPrefixBefore8192WindowUpper`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixBefore8192InStartWindow`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixBefore8192Candidate` | `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixBefore8192InStartWindow`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixBefore8192Candidate`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixBefore8192Candidate_mem_startWindow`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixBefore8192EqCandidate`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixBefore8192InStartWindow_of_eq_candidate`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixInCandidateWindow_of_before8192StartWindow`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixStep_abs_error_lt_unitRoundoff_mul_exactInput`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefix_abs_error_le_stdErrorEnvelope`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixBefore8192_le_inv_4096` | Records the remaining red bottleneck for the D1 reverse-order printed-value route. The exact earlier-block start-window theorem and the final `8192, ..., 4097` binade map are closed, and Lean now names the observed rounded earlier-block candidate `0x38fff94f` and proves that equality to it closes the start-window proposition. Lean has not yet proved that the actual rounded `10^9, ..., 8193` state equals that candidate or otherwise enters the same start window. The currently available standard-model accumulated-error envelope and the `1/4096` coarse rounded bound are too weak for the narrow finite-cell/window slack. The next proof route should be a dyadic finite-endpoint interval invariant over the rounded earlier block, not a billion-step Boolean certificate or per-step theorem expansion. |
| Higham Chapter 1 inverse-square scaled half-ulp no-change bridge | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/InstabilityWithoutCancellation.lean` | `inverseSquareSingleForwardStep`, `inverseSquareTerm`, `FloatingPointFormat.ieeeSingleFormat.normalizedValue` | `inverseSquareSingleForwardStep_normalizedValue_eq_self_of_scaled_half_ulp_at_scale` | Closed reusable dependency for the compressed §1.12.3 reverse high-prefix route. If the current positive binary32 state is in the exponent band encoded by `q` and `2^q < k^2`, Lean proves that adding `1/k^2` rounds back to the same state. This is the no-change companion to the scaled nearest-mantissa increment theorem; it supports a dyadic finite-endpoint invariant for `10^9, ..., 8193`, but does not by itself prove the remaining earlier-block equality. |

`inverseSquareSingleForwardAccumulator_4096_add_eq_plateau` | Packages the non-enumerative route for §1.12.3's forward summation prefix: Lean proves that every `1/k^2` with `2897 <= k < 4096` lies strictly between one half-ulp and one ulp at binary32 exponent `1`, then combines that interval with the adjacent-mantissa successor theorem and a recursive accumulator induction. Lean also certifies the early integer increment sum for terms `2, ..., 2896`, proves a bounded nearest-increment certificate for every early term, proves a source-rounding bridge from that certificate to the uniform early-increment rule, and proves the actual first step gives the binary32 value `1`. The local bridge theorems `inverseSquareSingleForwardStep_eq_left_of_adjacent_strict_between_left_closer` and `inverseSquareSingleForwardStep_eq_right_of_adjacent_strict_between_right_closer` factor the finite-normal-range and nearest/even adjacent-bracket boilerplate into reusable certificate targets; `inverseSquareSingleForwardStep_normalizedValue_nearest_mantissa_of_scaled_bounds_at_scale` then turns arbitrary exponent-band steps into integer half-cell checks with scale `2^q`. This closes the actual left-to-right forward first-stagnation trace without numeral-by-numeral predecessor proofs: Lean proves every intermediate prefix through `k = 4095` remains below the plateau, the `k = 4096` step enters the plateau, and every later term stays there for the actual accumulator from zero. Lean also models the reverse-order accumulator, proves the `10^9`-term run can be split into the high-index prefix and final `4096` low-index terms, and proves every high-prefix term index remains at least `4097`, hence each such term is bounded by `2^-24`. Remaining open work for §1.12.3 is the reported reverse-order numerical value and related summation-order examples. |

final `4096` low-index terms, proves the upper telescope `1/k^2 <= 1/(k-1)-1/k`, proves the lower telescope `1/k - 1/(k+1) <= 1/k^2`, and specializes both to show the exact high-prefix mass lies between the binary32 start-window endpoints and also below the refined tight upper endpoint `inverseSquareSingleReverseSuffixStartUpperTight`. The actual rounded high-prefix state is proved finite-system by `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixState_finiteSystem`, via the general reverse-accumulator induction `inverseSquareSingleReverseAccumulatorFrom_finiteSystem_of_start`; this supplies a finite-format premise for later nearest-cell/window certificates, but does not yet locate the state. The concrete binary32 candidate `0x397ff6b4` is named by `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixCandidate` and proved to lie in both the broad and tight suffix-start windows. The reusable left/right local rounding bridges `inverseSquareSingleForwardStep_eq_left_of_adjacent_strict_between_left_closer` and `inverseSquareSingleForwardStep_eq_right_of_adjacent_strict_between_right_closer` are the certificate/chunk interface for low-index suffix steps, `inverseSquareSingleForwardStep_normalizedValue_nearest_mantissa_of_scaled_bounds_at_scale` converts same-exponent suffix steps at arbitrary binary32 scale into integer half-cell checks, and `inverseSquareSingleReverseAccumulatorFrom_scaledBandPrefix_of_le` packages a whole same-exponent reverse band from those checked entries. The first concrete low-index suffix step is closed by `inverseSquareSingleReverseCandidate_add_4096_term_rounds_to_after4096`: adding `4096^{-2}` is exactly representable and lands on the named `0x3980035a` state. The second concrete suffix step is closed by `inverseSquareSingleReverseAfter4096_add_4095_term_rounds_to_after4095`: adding `4095^{-2}` rounds to the named `0x39800b5b` state using the arbitrary-exponent integer certificate with `q = 36`. The first same-exponent band after that is closed by `inverseSquareSingleReverseAfter4095Accumulator_4094_to_before2048`: a 2046-entry checked mantissa-prefix certificate proves that `4094^{-2}, ..., 2049^{-2}` carries the accumulator to `inverseSquareSingleReverseBefore2048Candidate` (`0x39ffef4f`). The `2048^{-2}` boundary step is closed by `inverseSquareSingleReverseBefore2048_add_2048_term_rounds_to_after2048`, landing on `inverseSquareSingleReverseAfter2048Candidate` (`0x3a0007a8`) by the nearest/even midpoint rule. The next band is closed by `inverseSquareSingleReverseAfter2048Accumulator_2047_to_before1024`: a 1023-entry checked certificate proves `2047^{-2}, ..., 1025^{-2}` carries the accumulator to `inverseSquareSingleReverseBefore1024Candidate` (`0x3a7fdfb6`). Subsequent compact continuation rows close the concrete candidate suffix as `inverseSquareSingleReverseCandidateSuffixMapsToPrinted_closed`; the remaining reverse-order bridge is the high-prefix equality certificate or a refined tight-window suffix certificate, not the low-index suffix trace. |
| Higham Chapter 1 reverse inverse-square high-prefix monotonicity and error envelope | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/InstabilityWithoutCancellation.lean` | `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_ge_left_of_finiteSystem_of_nonneg`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_abs_error_le_right_of_finiteSystem_of_nonneg`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_le_left_add_two_mul_right_of_finiteSystem_of_nonneg`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixState` | `inverseSquareSingleForwardStep_ge_self_of_finiteSystem`, `inverseSquareSingleForwardStep_abs_error_le_term_of_finiteSystem`, `inverseSquareSingleForwardStep_le_self_add_two_mul_term_of_finiteSystem`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseAccumulatorFrom_start_le_of_finiteSystem`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseAccumulatorFrom_nonneg_of_start_nonneg`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseAccumulatorFrom_le_start_add_two_mul_exact_zero_start`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixState_nonneg`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixState_le_two_mul_exact`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseTenPowNineHighPrefixState_le_inv_2048` | Adds reusable D1 invariants for the rounded high-prefix trace. The generic rounding lemmas prove that nearest finite round-to-even addition by a nonnegative quantity cannot return below the finite left input, has absolute error no larger than the added quantity, and increases the finite left input by at most twice the added quantity. The result lifts to inverse-square reverse accumulation, proving the actual `10^9, ..., 4097` high-prefix state is nonnegative and bounded by twice the exact high-prefix mass, hence by `1/2048`. This is not the final candidate-window proof, but it supplies a block-level error-recurrence envelope for later high-prefix interval certificates. |

The equality transfer says that if the rounded high-index prefix state equals the exact high-index prefix, Lean places it in the ordinary and tight suffix-start windows, and the full `10^9` reverse run follows from the corresponding final-suffix window certificate. The error-radius transfer defines the explicit tight-window margin around the exact high-prefix mass, proves it is nonnegative, and proves that any rounded high-prefix state within that absolute-error margin is enough for the refined tight-window route. The lower-bound layer replaces the exact-margin target by a fully explicit rational telescoping slack, proves that slack is nonnegative and below the exact margin, and composes it with the printed-value transfer theorem. This is a compact transfer theorem; it does not yet prove the rounded high-prefix error bound or a whole-window tight suffix map. |

`FloatingPointFormat.sourceRoundToEvenEvidence_normalizedValue_add_sameSign_sameExponent_error_finiteSystem`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_sameSign_sameExponent_error_finiteSystem` | Proves the source-side grid fact needed by the C4.4/FastTwoSum finite-error route: the exact sum of two same-sign, same-exponent normalized operands is `sign*(m+n)*beta^(e-t)` on the same exponent lattice, with coefficient `m+n < 2*beta^t`. When `m+n < beta^t`, the exact sum is finite representable and the operation-level finite round-to-even add returns it exactly. For the binary inexact guard-word branch, `binaryGuardQuotient_normalized_or_max_of_mantissaBound_le_of_lt_two_mul` dispatches the quotient to the ordinary `q,q+1` bracket or the max-mantissa boundary branch, `binaryGuardCoeffDiff_natAbs_lt_mantissaBound_of_floor_or_ceil` proves that choosing the lower quotient endpoint or the non-exact upper quotient endpoint gives a `t`-digit scaled error coefficient, the source-policy endpoint lemmas prove that actual round-to-even evidence inside a same-exponent adjacent bracket selects exactly one of the lower/upper mantissa indices `q` or `q+1`, and the positive and negative same-sign guard-word compositions construct the quotient brackets and directly discharge finite representability of the local roundoff error. The operand-level wrappers now derive the guard coefficient hypotheses directly from normalized operands and close source finite local-error representability for both signs via the sign-generic theorem. The operation-level wrapper transfers that aligned source finite-error witness to the concrete finite-normal rounded-add operation; the full rounded-add error theorem still has to extend this beyond the aligned same-sign case to the remaining alignment/sign/subnormal splits. |
| Finite-format normalized ordered-exponent exact and one-guard branches | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/FloatingPointArithmetic.lean` | `FloatingPointFormat.normalizedValue`, `FloatingPointFormat.normalizedMantissa`, `FloatingPointFormat.sourceRoundToEvenEvidence`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteSystem` | `FloatingPointFormat.normalizedValue_add_sameSign_orderedExponent_finiteSystem_of_alignedCoeff_lt_mantissaBound`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_sameSign_orderedExponent_eq_exact_of_alignedCoeff_lt_mantissaBound`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_sameSign_orderedExponent_error_finiteSystem_of_alignedCoeff_lt_mantissaBound`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_sameSign_orderedExponent_comm_eq_exact_of_alignedCoeff_lt_mantissaBound`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_sameSign_orderedExponent_comm_error_finiteSystem_of_alignedCoeff_lt_mantissaBound`, `FloatingPointFormat.sourceRoundToEvenEvidence_normalizedValue_add_sameSign_orderedExponent_error_finiteSystem_of_guardCoeffBounds`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_sameSign_orderedExponent_error_finiteSystem_of_guardCoeffBounds`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_sameSign_orderedExponent_comm_error_finiteSystem_of_guardCoeffBounds`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_sameSign_orderedExponent_error_finiteSystem_of_alignedCoeff_lt_two_mul_mantissaBound`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_sameSign_orderedExponent_comm_error_finiteSystem_of_alignedCoeff_lt_two_mul_mantissaBound` | Closes the same-sign normalized different-exponent exact-or-one-guard range. If the higher-exponent operand shifted to the lower exponent lattice plus the lower mantissa is below `beta^t`, both operand orders of concrete finite round-to-even add are exact with finite zero local error. If that aligned coefficient lies in the binary guard range `[beta^t, 2*beta^t)`, source round-to-even evidence feeds the existing guard-coefficient dispatcher and the finite-normal operation wrapper proves finite representability of the local roundoff error for both operand orders. The combined wrappers dispatch these two cases under the single hypothesis `alignedCoeff < 2*beta^t`. The remaining normalized different-exponent work is larger inexact alignment outside this range. |
| Finite-format opposite-sign same-exponent normalized addition exact branch | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/FloatingPointArithmetic.lean` | `FloatingPointFormat.normalizedValue`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteSystem` | `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_oppositeSign_sameExponent_eq_exact`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_oppositeSign_sameExponent_error_finiteSystem` | Closes the opposite-sign same-exponent normalized branch for the C4.4/FastTwoSum sign split. Adding an operand with flipped sign is rewritten to same-sign same-exponent subtraction; the existing finite-system subtraction theorem proves the exact source result is representable, so the concrete finite round-to-even add returns it exactly and the local roundoff error is the finite value zero. Remaining rounded-add work is in different-exponent alignment and mixed normal/subnormal boundary cases. |
| Finite-format all-subnormal addition exact branch | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/FloatingPointArithmetic.lean` | `FloatingPointFormat.subnormalValue`, `FloatingPointFormat.subnormalMantissa`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteSystem` | `FloatingPointFormat.subnormalValue_add_sameSign_finiteSystem_of_subnormalMantissas`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_sameSign_subnormal_eq_exact`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_sameSign_subnormal_error_finiteSystem`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_oppositeSign_subnormal_eq_exact`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_oppositeSign_subnormal_error_finiteSystem`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_subnormal_eq_exact`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_subnormal_error_finiteSystem` | Closes the all-subnormal C4.4/FastTwoSum addition split for arbitrary signs. Same-sign subnormal addition remains finite because the shared-lattice coefficient satisfies `m+n < beta^t`; opposite signs reduce to same-sign subnormal subtraction on the common lattice. The concrete finite round-to-even add is exact in all sign cases, so the local roundoff error is finite zero. Remaining rounded-add work is in normalized different-exponent alignment and mixed normal/subnormal boundary cases. |
| Finite-format mixed normal/subnormal exact and one-guard branches | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/FloatingPointArithmetic.lean` | `FloatingPointFormat.normalizedValue`, `FloatingPointFormat.subnormalValue`, `FloatingPointFormat.sourceRoundToEvenEvidence`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteSystem` | `FloatingPointFormat.normalizedValue_add_sameSign_subnormal_finiteSystem_of_alignedCoeff_lt_mantissaBound`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_normalized_sameSign_subnormal_eq_exact_of_alignedCoeff_lt_mantissaBound`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_normalized_sameSign_subnormal_error_finiteSystem_of_alignedCoeff_lt_mantissaBound`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_subnormal_sameSign_normalized_eq_exact_of_alignedCoeff_lt_mantissaBound`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_subnormal_sameSign_normalized_error_finiteSystem_of_alignedCoeff_lt_mantissaBound`, `FloatingPointFormat.sourceRoundToEvenEvidence_normalizedValue_add_sameSign_subnormal_error_finiteSystem_of_guardCoeffBounds`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_normalized_sameSign_subnormal_error_finiteSystem_of_guardCoeffBounds`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_subnormal_sameSign_normalized_error_finiteSystem_of_guardCoeffBounds`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_normalized_sameSign_subnormal_error_finiteSystem_of_alignedCoeff_lt_two_mul_mantissaBound`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_subnormal_sameSign_normalized_error_finiteSystem_of_alignedCoeff_lt_two_mul_mantissaBound` | Closes the same-sign mixed normal/subnormal exact-or-one-guard range. If shifting the normalized operand onto the subnormal lattice and adding the subnormal coefficient stays below `beta^t`, both operand orders of concrete finite round-to-even add are exact with finite zero local error. If that aligned coefficient lies in the binary guard range `[beta^t, 2*beta^t)`, source round-to-even evidence on the `emin` lattice feeds the existing guard-coefficient dispatcher and the finite-normal operation wrappers prove finite representability of the local roundoff error for both operand orders. The combined wrappers dispatch these two cases under the single hypothesis `alignedCoeff < 2*beta^t`. The remaining mixed work is alignment outside this range. |
| Finite-format normalized Sterbenz add-neg exact branch | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/FloatingPointArithmetic.lean` | `FloatingPointFormat.normalizedSystem`, `FloatingPointFormat.sterbenzRatioCondition`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteSystem` | `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_neg_right_normalizedSystem_eq_exact_of_sterbenzRatioCondition`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_neg_right_normalizedSystem_error_finiteSystem_of_sterbenzRatioCondition`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_neg_right_positive_normalizedValue_eq_exact_of_sterbenzRatioCondition`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_neg_right_positive_normalizedValue_error_finiteSystem_of_sterbenzRatioCondition` | Packages the opposite-sign normalized addition branch as Sterbenz subtraction: if normalized `x` and `y` satisfy `y/2 < x < 2*y`, then the concrete finite round-to-even operation for `x + (-y)` returns the exact real sum, and the local roundoff error is finite zero. The positive normalized-value specialization exposes the common C4.4 branch `normalizedValue false m e + normalizedValue true n e'`. This closes exact normalized opposite-sign add cases where the Sterbenz ratio is available; deriving that ratio from the remaining FastTwoSum alignment/magnitude split remains open. |
| Finite-format finite-system Sterbenz add-neg exact branch | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/FloatingPointArithmetic.lean` | `FloatingPointFormat.finiteSystem`, `FloatingPointFormat.sterbenzRatioCondition`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp` | `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_neg_right_finiteSystem_eq_exact_of_sterbenzRatioCondition`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_neg_right_finiteSystem_error_finiteSystem_of_sterbenzRatioCondition`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_neg_left_finiteSystem_eq_exact_of_sterbenzRatioCondition`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_neg_left_finiteSystem_error_finiteSystem_of_sterbenzRatioCondition` | Extends the Sterbenz add-neg exact branch from normalized operands to arbitrary finite operands, including normal, subnormal, and mixed cases. The wrappers reuse the full finite-system Sterbenz subtraction theorem to prove that `x + (-y)` and the commuted form `(-y) + x` round exactly, so both local roundoff errors are finite zero. This closes the finite-system/subnormal exact-add branch once a `sterbenzRatioCondition` is available; deriving that condition from the remaining FastTwoSum base-2 split remains open. |

| Higham Chapter 1 reverse inverse-square after-`32` through final concrete suffix closure | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/InstabilityWithoutCancellation.lean` | `inverseSquareSingleReverseAfter32Candidate`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseBefore16Candidate`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseAfter16Candidate`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseBefore8Candidate`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseAfter8Candidate`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseBefore4Candidate`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseAfter4Candidate`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseAfter3Candidate`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseAfter2Candidate` | `inverseSquareSingleReverseBefore32_add_32_term_rounds_to_after32`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseAfter32Prefix_31_to_17_eq`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseAfter32Band31To17CertificateBool_eq_true`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseAfter32Accumulator_31_to_before16`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseBefore16_add_16_term_rounds_to_after16`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseAfter16Prefix_15_to_9_eq`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseAfter16Band15To9CertificateBool_eq_true`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseAfter16Accumulator_15_to_before8`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseBefore8_add_8_term_rounds_to_after8`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseAfter8Prefix_7_to_5_eq`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseAfter8Band7To5CertificateBool_eq_true`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseAfter8Accumulator_7_to_before4`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseBefore4SuffixMapsToPrinted_closed`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseBefore32SuffixMapsToPrinted_closed`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseCandidateSuffixMapsToPrinted_closed`, `inverseSquareSingleReverseAccumulator_ten_pow_nine_eq_printed_of_highPrefix_eq_candidate` | Closes the concrete low-index suffix completely. The `32^{-2}` and `8^{-2}` boundary steps are exact representable transitions, the `16^{-2}` boundary is a midpoint/even transition, and compact checked certificates discharge `31^{-2}, ..., 17^{-2}`, `15^{-2}, ..., 9^{-2}`, and `7^{-2}, 6^{-2}, 5^{-2}`. The final four additions `4^{-2}, 3^{-2}, 2^{-2}, 1^{-2}` are then closed directly, including the rounded `3^{-2}` step by an integer half-cell certificate. This proves the concrete candidate suffix maps to Higham's printed binary32 reverse accumulator; the remaining reverse-order gap is the high-prefix equality or refined tight-window bridge. |

Also records the displayed base-two, three-digit example: exact difference `1/8`, dropped-guard result `1/4`, factor-two overshoot, and relative error `1`. This is the abstract no-guard model and example arithmetic, not the Ferguson/Sterbenz guard-digit exact-subtraction theorem. |

`FloatingPointFormat.sterbenzRatioCondition_symm`, `FloatingPointFormat.sterbenzRatioCondition_abs_sub_lt_left`, `FloatingPointFormat.sterbenzRatioCondition_abs_sub_lt_right`, `FloatingPointFormat.sterbenzRatioCondition_abs_sub_lt_min`, `FloatingPointFormat.decimalSingleDigitFormat_normalizedExponentRepresentation_four`, `FloatingPointFormat.decimalSingleDigitFormat_normalizedExponentRepresentation_five`, `FloatingPointFormat.decimalSingleDigitFormat_normalizedExponentRepresentation_nine`, `FloatingPointFormat.decimalSingleDigitFormat_sterbenzRatioCondition_nine_five`, `FloatingPointFormat.decimalSingleDigitFormat_not_fergusonExponentCondition_nine_five`, `FloatingPointFormat.decimalSingleDigitFormat_sterbenzRatio_not_ferguson`, `FloatingPointFormat.sterbenzFergusonBridgeCondition_ratio`, `FloatingPointFormat.sterbenzFergusonBridgeCondition_ferguson`, `FloatingPointFormat.guardDigitSubtractionModel_exact_of_sterbenzBridge` | Names the exact theorem surface for Higham Theorem 2.4 and the current Sterbenz bridge: explicit normalized exponent representations model `e(x)`, the Ferguson condition includes a normalized representation of `x-y` to expose the no-underflow/no-overflow side condition, the finite-format proof derives that the exponents of `x` and `y` differ by at most one, that the operands have same-sign normalized representations under Ferguson's condition, and that the remaining exponent cases are same exponent or one-exponent shift. The same-exponent and one-exponent-shift raw aligned subtraction identities are formalized; same-exponent mantissa subtraction has at most `t` digits and is preserved by the modeled `t`-digit coefficient rounding; the same-exponent exact difference is a finite normalized value when the integer mantissa difference is already normalized; the one-base-shift and arbitrary finite radix-power shift same-exponent exact differences are finite normalized when the shifted integer difference is normalized and the shifted exponent is in range; exact zero is finite; the same-exponent exact difference is finite at `emin` when that integer difference is below the normalized leading-digit threshold, and the shifted `emin` subnormal endpoint is finite when a finite radix-power shift lands below that threshold; `sameExponentFiniteDifferenceWitness` packages exact zero, normalized-shift, and shifted `emin` subnormal endpoint cases behind one finite-system wrapper, and `sameExponentFiniteDifferenceWitness_of_normalizedMantissas` derives that witness from same-exponent normalized operand mantissas and an in-range exponent; and the one-shift guard-aligned coefficient, including the integer `beta*mHigh - mLow` form, is proved below the normalized leading-digit threshold under both adjacent orientations. The leading digit `z1` of the `t+1` guard word is proved zero, the trailing `t`-digit tail is unchanged, dropping that zero guard digit with the original sign gives the exact adjacent-exponent subtraction value, and the same-exponent/high-minus-low/low-minus-high branch cases are packaged by `guardDigitBranchSubtractionModel`. The noncomputable evidence-selecting `guardDigitBranchSubtractionRoutine` is proved to satisfy `guardDigitSubtractionModel`, exactness, and finite-system output under Ferguson's condition; the branch-selected value is finite with the same-exponent branch discharged by the derived selector; and concrete finite round-to-even subtraction is exact for same-exponent normalized operands, positive normalized, all-subnormal, mixed normal/subnormal, and all finite Sterbenz operands, and Ferguson branch data. The Sterbenz ratio distance lemmas, the direct positive adjacent coefficient bound, the symmetric ratio and positive normalized exponent-gap lemmas, the positive normalized and normalized-system finite-system branches, the same-sign subnormal lattice branch, and exact finite round-to-even positive normalized, all-subnormal, mixed normal/subnormal, and finite-system all-case subtraction are proved, and a one-digit decimal counterexample shows Sterbenz's ratio condition does not imply Ferguson's exponent condition in general bases. A fully executable digit-level/full IEEE implementation and the corresponding full IEEE operation theorem remain open. |

The expanded radicand is factored into the exact Kahan radicand times nine unit-roundoff-bounded local relative-error factors; the direct finite-normal-range theorem proves the closed relative-error bound `(1 + gamma 9) * (1 + u)^2 - 1` for the modeled no-underflow trace. |
| Higham Chapter 2 logarithmic leading-digit distribution (§2.5) | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/LeadingDigitDistribution.lean` | `leadingDigitOfIndex`, `logarithmicLeadingDigitMass`, `logarithmicIntervalMass`, `logarithmicLeadingDigitProbability` | `leadingDigitOfIndex_pos`, `leadingDigitOfIndex_lt_base`, `logarithmicLeadingDigitMass_eq_log_div`, `logarithmicLeadingDigitMass_eq_log_one_add_inv`, `logarithmicLeadingDigitMass_eq_intervalMass`, `logarithmicIntervalMass_mul_base_pow`, `logarithmicIntervalMass_mul_base_zpow`, `logarithmicLeadingDigitMass_scaled_bin`, `logarithmicLeadingDigitMass_scaled_bin_zpow`, `logarithmicLeadingDigitMass_nonneg`, `logarithmicLeadingDigitMass_succ_lt`, `sum_logarithmicLeadingDigitMass_eq_one`, `logarithmicLeadingDigitProbability_prob_eq_log_div`, `logarithmicLeadingDigitProbability_prob_eq_log_one_add_inv`, `decimalLogarithmicLeadingDigitProbability_prob_eq`, `decimalLogarithmicLeadingDigitProbability_prob_eq_log_one_add_inv`, `decimalLogarithmicLeadingDigitProbability_first_gt_last`, `decimalLogarithmicLeadingDigitProbability_nonuniform` | Formalizes Higham's displayed logarithmic/Benford leading-digit law.  For any base `beta > 1`, the mass assigned to leading digit `n` is proved equal to both `log_beta ((n+1)/n)` and the source form `log_beta (1+1/n)`, each atom is nonnegative, adjacent masses strictly decrease as the leading digit increases, and the masses over digits `1, ..., beta-1` telescope to one, yielding a repository-native finite probability law.  The decimal specialization proves the same `1+1/n` probability formula and that digit `1` has strictly larger probability than digit `9`, so the decimal law is not uniform, matching the source discussion and table.  The interval-mass theorem proves the source-facing scale-invariance algebra: multiplying a positive bin by a natural or integer power of the base leaves its logarithmic mass unchanged.  Brent's base-optimality theorem, product-convergence explanations, `q^k` equidistribution, and empirical examples remain open. |
| Higham Chapter 2 statistical rounding-error square-root rule (§2.6) | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/StatisticalRounding.lean` | `statisticalRoundingErrorSum`, `StatisticalRoundingErrorModel` | `StatisticalRoundingErrorModel.expectation_sum_eq_zero`, `StatisticalRoundingErrorModel.expectation_sum_sq_eq_sum_second_moments`, `StatisticalRoundingErrorModel.expectation_sum_sq_le_card_mul_unit_sq`, `StatisticalRoundingErrorModel.rms_sum_le_sqrt_card_mul_unit` | Formalizes the finite second-moment theorem behind Higham's `sqrt n` rule of thumb.  Under explicit zero-mean, pairwise-uncorrelated, and per-step second-moment `<= u^2` assumptions, the accumulated rounding-error sum has mean zero, its second moment is the sum of the individual second moments, that second moment is at most `n*u^2`, and its RMS size is at most `sqrt n * u`.  This is a theorem under visible statistical assumptions; concrete random rounding, CLT/asymptotic normality, PRECISE/CESTAC, and logarithmic-mantissa models remain open/descriptive. |
| Higham Chapter 2 level-index alternative number system (§2.7) | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/AlternativeNumberSystems.lean` | `levelIndexForward`, `levelIndexBackward`, `LevelIndexCode`, `LevelIndexCode.index`, `LevelIndexCode.value`, `LevelIndexCode.reciprocalValue` | `levelIndexForward_succ`, `levelIndexBackward_succ`, `levelIndexBackward_forward`, `LevelIndexCode.frac_mem_unit_interval`, `LevelIndexCode.backward_value_eq_frac`, `LevelIndexCode.value_pos_of_pos_level`, `LevelIndexCode.reciprocalValue_pos_of_pos_level`, `LevelIndexCode.reciprocalValue_mul_value_of_pos_level`, `LevelIndexCode.value_mul_reciprocalValue_of_pos_level` | Formalizes the precise level-index representation described in §2.7.  A code stores a natural level and a fractional part in `[0,1]`; the positive-side value is `exp` iterated `level` times on the fraction; iterating `log` the same number of times recovers the fraction; and the reciprocal-side decoder gives the `(0,1)` branch.  The text's range/cost/comparison claims and other alternative systems remain descriptive/bibliographic. |
| Higham Chapter 2 accuracy-test exact baselines (§2.8) | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/AccuracyTests.lean` | `unitRoundoffProbeExact`, `codySineTestExact`, `codySineReducedArgument`, `codySineDisplayedTableMagnitude17`, `codySineDisplayedTableDecimal17`, `sineTaylorOdd5`, `codyPowerBase`, `codyPowerExponent`, `codyPowerTestExact`, `codyPowerExpLogPath`, `codyPowerDisplayedDecimal21`, `codyPowerDisplayedTableDecimal17`, `karpinskiGuardDigitProbeA`, `karpinskiGuardDigitProbeB`, `karpinskiGuardDigitFiniteProbeA`, `karpinskiGuardDigitFiniteProbeB` | `unitRoundoffProbeExact_eq_zero`, `codySineReducedArgument_pos`, `codySineReducedArgument_lt_one_hundredth`, `codySineReducedArgument_abs_lt_one_hundredth`, `codySineTestExact_eq_neg_sin_reducedArgument`, `codySineTestExact_neg`, `codySineTestExact_abs_lt_one_hundredth`, `sineTaylorOdd5_eq`, `sineTaylorOdd5_abs_error_le_next`, `codySineReducedArgument_sineTaylorOdd5_abs_error_lt_one_e20`, `codySineTestExact_sineTaylorOdd5_abs_error_lt_one_e20`, `codySineTaylorOdd5_displayedMagnitude_abs_error_lt_41e21`, `codySineTestExact_displayedTableDecimal17_abs_error_lt_half_last_place`, `codyPowerBase_pos`, `codyPowerExpLogPath_eq_exact`, `codyPowerTestExact_displayedDecimal21_abs_error_lt_half_last_place`, `codyPowerTestExact_displayedTableDecimal17_abs_error_lt_half_last_place`, `exp_absolute_error_relative_error_eq`, `exp_absolute_error_relative_error_abs_lt_101_mul_abs`, `karpinskiGuardDigitProbeA_eq_zero`, `karpinskiGuardDigitProbeB_eq_zero`, `karpinskiGuardDigitProbes_equal`, `FloatingPointFormat.decimalOneDigitThreeExponent_karpinskiProbeA_eq_neg_one_tenth`, `FloatingPointFormat.decimalOneDigitThreeExponent_karpinskiProbeB_eq_neg_one_tenth`, `FloatingPointFormat.decimalOneDigitThreeExponent_karpinskiProbes_equal` | Records exact real-arithmetic baselines for §2.8.  The Table 2.3 unit-roundoff probe is zero in exact arithmetic; Cody's sine test is reduced by the certified identity `sin(22) = -sin(22-7*pi)`, with `0 < 22-7*pi < 0.01`, hence `sin(22) < 0` and `|sin(22)| < 0.01`; the five-term odd Taylor polynomial has certified reduced-argument error below `10^-20`, the rational interval bridge bounds the Taylor value against the Table 2.4 exact-row magnitude, and Lean certifies the signed displayed decimal for `sin(22)` to within one half of the last shown place; Cody's `2.5^125` test agrees exactly with `exp(125*log(2.5))`; Lean certifies both displayed exact power decimals for `2.5^125` to within one half of the last shown place; the exponentiation sensitivity identity proves `(exp(w+deltaW)-exp w)/exp w = exp(deltaW)-1`, and the small-error theorem bounds its absolute value by `1.01*|deltaW|` when `|deltaW| < 0.01`; the two Karpinski guard-digit probes are equal in exact arithmetic, and the one-digit decimal finite round-to-even trace computes both operation paths as `-0.1`.  Historical machine outputs and full guard-digit diagnostic/test-harness semantics remain open. |
| Higham Chapter 2 finite single-rounding FMA (§2.9) | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/FusedMultiplyAdd.lean` | `fusedMultiplyAddExact`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenFMA`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToModeFMA` | `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToModeFMA_nearestEven`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenFMA_eq_round_exact`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenFMA_eq_exact_of_finiteSystem`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenFMA_standardModel_lt_of_finiteNormalRange`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenFMA_inverseRelErrorWitness_of_finiteNormalRange` | Formalizes the finite real-valued surface of the §2.9 FMA note: `x*y+z` is formed as one exact real quantity and then rounded once by the finite selector.  Exact representable fused results are fixed, and finite-normal fused results satisfy the strict standard-model and inverse-relative-error witnesses.  Full IEEE FMA semantics with flags, signed zeros, infinities, NaNs, traps, payloads, and hardware details remain open. |
| Higham Chapter 2 Problem 2.1 finite-format counts | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/Counting.lean` | `FloatingPointFormat.normalizedExponentParameterCount`, `FloatingPointFormat.normalizedMantissaParameterCount`, `FloatingPointFormat.subnormalMantissaParameterCount`, `FloatingPointFormat.signedParameterCount`, `FloatingPointFormat.normalizedNumberParameterCount`, `FloatingPointFormat.subnormalNumberParameterCount` | `FloatingPointFormat.normalizedMantissaParameterCount_eq_beta_pow_sub`, `FloatingPointFormat.subnormalMantissaParameterCount_eq_beta_pow_sub_one`, `FloatingPointFormat.normalizedNumberParameterCount_eq_problem2_1_formula`, `FloatingPointFormat.subnormalNumberParameterCount_eq_problem2_1_formula`, `FloatingPointFormat.subnormalValue_eq_iff_sign_mantissa`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_1_ieeeSingle_normalizedNumberParameterCount`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_1_ieeeSingle_subnormalNumberParameterCount`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_1_ieeeDouble_normalizedNumberParameterCount`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_1_ieeeDouble_subnormalNumberParameterCount` | Closes Problem 2.1 for the repository's inclusive finite-format model.  The signed nonzero normalized count is `2 * (emax - emin + 1) * (beta^t - beta^(t-1))`, the signed nonzero subnormal count is `2 * (beta^(t-1) - 1)`, IEEE single gives `4,261,412,864` normalized and `16,777,214` subnormal values, and IEEE double gives `18,428,729,675,200,069,632` normalized and `9,007,199,254,740,990` subnormal values.  Normalized representation uniqueness was already available through `normalizedValue_eq_iff_sign_exp_mantissa`; this row adds the analogous subnormal sign/mantissa uniqueness theorem.  Full IEEE encodings, signed zeros, infinities, NaNs, exception flags, and traps remain outside this finite-format count. |
| Higham Chapter 2 Problem 2.2 / Lemma 2.1 spacing proof | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/Problem2_2.lean`, `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/FloatingPointArithmetic.lean` | `FloatingPointFormat.realOrderAdjacentNormalized`, `FloatingPointFormat.machineEpsilon` | `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_2_lemma2_1_spacing_bounds_left`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_2_lemma2_1_spacing_bounds_right`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_2_lemma2_1_spacing_bounds`, `FloatingPointFormat.realOrderAdjacentNormalized_spacing_bounds_left` | Exposes the exercise-level proof of Lemma 2.1.  For real-order adjacent normalized endpoints `x` and `y`, the spacing satisfies `beta^(-1) * eps_M * |x| <= |x-y| <= eps_M * |x|`, and the wrapper also records the symmetric version using `|y|` as the reference endpoint.  The proof reuses the structural-to-real-order adjacency classification already proved in the finite-format foundation.  This is a normalized finite-format spacing result, not a full IEEE special-value or subnormal theorem. |
| Higham Chapter 2 Problem 2.7 finite exercise facts | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/Problem2_7.lean` | `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp`, `BasicOp.add`, `BasicOp.sub`, `BasicOp.mul`, `BasicOp.div`, `FloatingPointFormat.decimalOneDigitTwoExponentFormat` | `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_comm`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_mul_comm`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_7_statement2_sub_sign_symmetry_of_exact_finiteSystem`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_7_statement2_sub_sign_symmetry_of_not_finiteNormalRange`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_7_statement2_sub_sign_symmetry`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_add_self_eq_mul_two`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp_half_mul_eq_div_two`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_7_statement5_add_associativity_false`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_7_statement6_midpoint_strict_between_false` | Closes the ordinary finite round-to-even versions of Problem 2.7 statements 1, 3, and 4: addition/multiplication commute before the final rounding, `a+a` and `2*a` produce the same exact real input to rounding, and `(1/2)*a` and `a/2` do likewise.  It also closes statement 2 for the total finite round-to-even selector under even radix and `1 < t`, after keeping the exact-representable and off-finite-normal branch lemmas as reusable facts.  Statement 5 is refuted by rounded-addition nonassociativity and statement 6 by the one-digit decimal midpoint example.  Full IEEE operation classification with exceptions/special values remains open. |
| Higham Chapter 2 Problem 2.8 decimal midpoint strict-between violation and guard-digit audit | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/Midpoint.lean` | `FloatingPointFormat.decimalOneDigitTwoExponentFormat`, `FloatingPointFormat.decimalOneDigitThreeExponentFormat`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEven`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp` | `FloatingPointFormat.decimalOneDigitTwoExponentFormat_finiteSystem_one`, `FloatingPointFormat.decimalOneDigitTwoExponentFormat_finiteSystem_two`, `FloatingPointFormat.decimalOneDigitTwoExponent_midpoint_one_two_rounds_to_two`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_8_decimal_midpoint_strict_between_violated`, `FloatingPointFormat.decimalOneDigitThreeExponentFormat_finiteSystem_one_half`, `FloatingPointFormat.decimalOneDigitThreeExponent_midpoint_one_two_rounds_to_two`, `FloatingPointFormat.decimalOneDigitThreeExponent_sub_two_one_exact`, `FloatingPointFormat.decimalOneDigitThreeExponent_div_one_two_exact`, `FloatingPointFormat.decimalOneDigitThreeExponent_add_one_one_half_rounds_to_two`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_8_finiteRoundToEven_guarded_sequence_counterexample`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_8_guarded_sequence_counterexample_missing_midpoint_finiteSystem`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_8_exact_guarded_midpoint_strict_between`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_8_guarded_exact_operation_sequence_eq_exact_midpoint`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_8_guarded_exact_operation_sequence_strict_between`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_8_guarded_sequence_eq_exact_midpoint_of_finite_midpoint_steps`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_8_guarded_sequence_strict_between_of_finite_midpoint_steps`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_8_guarded_sequence_strict_between_of_sterbenz_subtraction` | Closes the first sentence of Problem 2.8 with a concrete base-10 finite round-to-even counterexample: in the one-digit decimal format, `1` and `2` are floating-point numbers with `1 < 2`, but `fl((1+2)/2) = fl(3/2) = 2`, so `1 < fl((1+2)/2) < 2` is false.  It also audits the second sentence by proving that the naive finite round-to-even operation-sequence interpretation is insufficient: in the one-digit decimal format with `1/2` representable, `2-1` and division by `2` are exact, but the final rounded addition `1+1/2` returns the endpoint `2`, so the strict inequality fails.  The exact real theorem proves `a < a+(b-a)/2 < b`; the exact-operation wrappers prove that any operation model with exact subtraction, exact halving, and exact final addition computes that interior midpoint.  The corrected finite-selector theorem first proves the rounded operation path equals that exact midpoint once `b-a`, `(b-a)/2`, and `a+(b-a)/2` are all finite representable, then derives the strict inequality from the exact theorem.  The Sterbenz-specialized theorem discharges exact subtraction from finite endpoints plus Sterbenz's ratio condition, leaving the half-difference and final-midpoint representability hypotheses explicit.  The remaining lift is proving that a concrete full guard-digit/IEEE operation implementation supplies the exact-step hypotheses; the real-arithmetic exact-operation theorem surface is closed. |
| Higham Chapter 2 finite double-rounding counterexample (§2.3 / Problem 2.9) | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/DoubleRounding.lean` | `FloatingPointFormat.binaryT2DoubleRoundingDestinationFormat`, `FloatingPointFormat.binaryT3DoubleRoundingExtendedFormat`, `FloatingPointFormat.binary64MantissaExtendedLocalFormat`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_9Source`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEven`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenSqrt` | `FloatingPointFormat.binaryT3DoubleRounding_rounds_21_16_to_5_4`, `FloatingPointFormat.binaryT2DoubleRounding_rounds_5_4_to_1`, `FloatingPointFormat.binaryT2DoubleRounding_rounds_21_16_to_3_2`, `FloatingPointFormat.binaryDoubleRounding_counterexample`, `FloatingPointFormat.binaryT2DoubleRounding_neg_three_sixteenths_not_finiteSystem`, `FloatingPointFormat.binaryT2DoubleRounding_roundoff_error_not_finiteSystem`, `FloatingPointFormat.binaryT2DoubleRounding_21_16_finiteNormalRange`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteNormalRange_not_enough_for_roundoff_error_finiteSystem`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_9_direct_double_rounds_to_predecessor`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_9_direct_double_sqrt_rounds_to_predecessor`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_9_extended64_rounds_to_double_midpoint`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_9_double_rounds_extended_midpoint_to_one`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_9_double_rounding_from_extended64`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_9_direct_double_ne_double_rounded_extended64` | Gives a finite binary round-to-even witness for Higham's warning that extended-precision double rounding can differ from direct destination rounding: `21/16` rounds to `5/4` in the `t = 3` format, then to `1` in the `t = 2` format, while direct `t = 2` rounding gives `3/2`.  The same tiny format also proves a route-failure fact useful for Chapter 4: `21/16` is inside the `t = 2` finite-normal range, but its direct rounding error is `-3/16`, which is not finite representable; therefore finite-normal range alone cannot prove roundoff-error representability without finite binary operand hypotheses.  It also closes Problem 2.9's finite selector calculation for `sqrt(1 - 2^-53)`: direct IEEE-double finite round-to-even gives `1 - 2^-53`, while first rounding to the local 64-bit-mantissa extended format gives the double midpoint `1 - 2^-54` and the final double rounding ties to even at `1`.  Full IEEE operation semantics with traps, signaling NaNs/payloads, and all special values remain open. |

Lean composes the Nat reciprocal-cell and midpoint certificates with this final product threshold for both left and right adjacent reciprocal endpoints, specializes the success branch to the quotient `floor(2^105/(2^52+j))`, discharges the quotient mantissa-range, nonzero-remainder, and no-exact-half-tie hypotheses automatically from `0 < j < 257736490`, reduces the midpoint side to comparing `2*((2^105) % (2^52+j))` with `2^52+j`, reduces the left scaled-product check to `((2^105) % (2^52+j)) <= 2^51`, and proves the right scaled-product check automatically.  The original remainder is also rewritten through `(2*j^2) % (2^52+j)`, the small quotient `floor(2*j^2/(2^52+j))` is proved at most `29`, the top quotient band `= 29` is proved to round back to `1`, the lower quotient bands `<= 28` are closed by the left-branch threshold theorem, and the final all-earlier wrapper proves every positive `j < 257736490` rounds back to `1`. |

Lean proves `x_j = (2^52 + j)*2^-52`, converts the scaled integer-cell inequalities `k*(2^52+j) < 2^105 < (k+1)*(2^52+j)` into the strict real interval around `1/x_j`, exposes the same conversion directly from Nat inequalities, and names the quotient `floor(2^105/(2^52+j))`, where a nonzero remainder gives the strict adjacent reciprocal cell for that quotient. The local reference convention returns common quiet/default results, including `0^0 = 1` with no flags, while `problem2_15_16_probe_not_forced_by_core_ieee_model` proves that the repository's core IEEE primitive-operation model leaves every such elementary probe unconstrained unless an implementation-specific elementary library is added.  The empirical "test any environment" aspect and language/library-specific behavior remain separate. |
| Higham Chapter 2 Problem 2.17 rounded discriminant sign counterexample | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/Problem2_17.lean` | `problem2_17_discriminant`, `problem2_17_computedDiscriminant` | `problem2_17_true_discriminant_nonnegative`, `problem2_17_true_discriminant_eq_one_tenth`, `problem2_17_computed_discriminant_negative`, `problem2_17_standard_model_witness_exact_values`, `problem2_17_standard_model_counterexample`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_17_decimalOneDigitThreeExponentFormat_finiteSystem_nine_tenths`, `problem2_17_standard_model_counterexample_with_decimal_finite_inputs` | Proves that the source's phenomenon can occur in the standard model for the rounded path `fl(fl(b*b) - fl(a*c))` used before computing `sqrt(b^2-a*c)` in `a*x^2 - 2*b*x + c = 0`.  With `a = 1`, `b = 1`, and `c = 9/10`, the true value `b^2 - a*c` is exactly `1/10`, while a valid abstract `FPModel` with `u = 1/10` computes rounded discriminant `-9/100`.  The strengthened witness proves the inputs are finite values of the one-digit decimal format.  The rounded operations still come from the abstract model; a concrete hardware/finite-operation trace remains open. |
| Higham Chapter 2 Problem 2.18 exponent-gap strengthening counterexample | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/Problem2_18.lean` | `problem2_18_positiveExponentGapAtMostOne`, `FloatingPointFormat.decimalOneDigitTwoExponentFormat`, `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenOp`, `BasicOp.sub` | `problem2_18_twenty_one_exponent_gap`, `problem2_18_nineteen_finiteNormalRange`, `problem2_18_sub_twenty_one_rounds_to_twenty`, `problem2_18_nineteen_not_finiteSystem`, `problem2_18_source_counterexample_exact_values`, `problem2_18_exponent_gap_not_sufficient_for_exact_subtraction` | Refutes the proposed strengthening of Theorem 2.4 that would use positivity plus exponent gap at most one as a sufficient condition for exact subtraction.  In the one-digit decimal format, `20` and `1` are positive normalized finite values with exponents `2` and `1`; the exact difference `19` is in the normal range but is not representable, and the finite round-to-even subtraction returns `20`, not `19`.  The packaged source theorem records the exponent-gap condition, exact difference, normal-range/nonrepresentability facts, rounded value, and exact-subtraction failure in one statement.  This closes the finite-selector counterexample; full hardware/IEEE operation semantics remain separate. |
| Higham Chapter 2 Problem 2.19 square-root identity requirements | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/Problem2_19.lean` | `FloatingPointFormat.finiteRoundToEvenSqrt`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_19_roundedSqrtSquare`, `FloatingPointFormat.decimalOneDigitThreeExponentFormat` | `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_19_sqrt_square_eq_abs_of_finiteSystem`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_19_decimalOneDigitThreeExponent_sqrt_two_rounds_to_one`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_19_decimalOneDigitThreeExponent_roundedSqrtSquare_two_eq_one`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_19_roundedSqrtSquare_not_abs_counterexample`, `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_19_first_requirement_holds_second_fails` | Separates the two source requirements for a square-root routine.  The reasonable identity is closed at the finite selector layer: if `x` is finite, then `finiteRoundToEvenSqrt (x^2) = |x|`, because the exact square-root result is representable.  The rounded-root-then-square identity is refuted in the one-digit decimal format: `fl(sqrt(2)) = 1`, so squaring the rounded result returns `1`, not `|2|`.  The answer theorem packages both sides in one source-facing statement.  Full IEEE special-value/flag semantics remain separate. |

`FloatingPointFormat.problem2_20_decimalOneDigitThreeExponent_trace_exceeds_one_but_fails_source_audit` | Proves the source Euclidean baseline `|x / sqrt(x^2+y^2)| <= 1` and the one-sided `x / sqrt(x^2+y^2) <= 1`, then gives both a source-shaped standard-model counterexample and a concrete finite round-to-even selector trace for the naive rounded computation.  For `x = 11/10` and `y = 0`, the exact ratio is `1`, but a valid `FPModel` with `u = 1/5` computes `11/10 > 1`; the strengthened standard-model theorem proves these inputs are finite values of a local two-digit decimal format.  For the one-digit decimal finite-selector trace, `x = 3/2` and `y = 0` have exact ratio `1`, while the rounded path sends `(3/2)^2` to `2`, keeps `0^2` and `2+0` exact, rounds `sqrt(2)` to `1`, and rounds `(3/2)/1` to `2`; `problem2_20_decimalOneDigitThreeExponent_three_halves_not_finiteSystem` proves this is not yet a finite-input trace, and `FloatingPointFormat.problem2_20_decimalOneDigitThreeExponent_exact_trace_range_audit` proves the nonzero exact intermediates are finite-normal while the exact zero square is in the finite underflow range.  The named no-square-underflow predicate and component theorem prove any no-square-underflow witness must have nonzero components, and the packaged trace audit theorem proves the existing one-digit trace fails both finite-input and no-square-underflow requirements.  A concrete finite-selector or IEEE instruction trace with finite inputs, nonzero components, and the source's no-overflow/no-underflow side condition remains open, as do full IEEE instruction semantics with flags, traps, and special values. |
| Higham Chapter 2 Problem 2.21 naive IEEE maximum branch | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/Problem2_21.lean` | `IeeeValue`, `IeeeValue.ieeeGt`, `ieeeNaiveMax` | `ieeeNaiveMax_eq_left_of_ieeeGt`, `ieeeNaiveMax_eq_right_of_not_ieeeGt`, `ieeeNaiveMax_finite_finite_left_of_lt`, `ieeeNaiveMax_finite_finite_right_of_le`, `ieeeNaiveMax_finite_finite_eq_max`, `ieeeNaiveMax_left_nan`, `ieeeNaiveMax_right_nan`, `ieeeNaiveMax_nan_finite`, `ieeeNaiveMax_finite_nan`, `ieeeNaiveMax_nan_finite_ne_finite_nan`, `ieeeNaiveMax_left_nan_finite_result_not_nan`, `ieeeNaiveMax_concrete_nan_counterexample`, `ieeeNaiveMax_not_nan_propagating`, `ieeeNaiveMax_finite_correct_but_not_nan_propagating` | Formalizes the literal source code `if x > y then x else y` over the repository's modeled IEEE value/comparison layer.  On finite real operands Lean proves that the branch returns `max x y`.  Since modeled IEEE greater-than is false when either operand is NaN, Lean also proves that `ieeeNaiveMax NaN y = y` and `ieeeNaiveMax x NaN = NaN`; in particular a left NaN with finite right operand is discarded, while a right NaN is returned.  The branch is therefore not symmetric in NaN cases and does not satisfy the common "propagate any NaN input" expectation.  The packaged theorem states finite-input correctness and NaN-propagation failure together.  This closes the Problem 2.21 code-path counterexample, not full IEEE max/min instruction semantics. |
| Higham Chapter 2 Problem 2.22 Kahan Heron guard-digit accuracy | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/Problem2_22.lean`, `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/Heron.lean` | `kahanOrderedTriangleSides`, `finiteKahanHeronAB`, `finiteKahanHeronArea`, `kahanHeronArea` | `problem2_22_guard_digit_a_sub_b_exact`, `problem2_22_kahanHeronArea_relError_le_gamma9_unitRoundoff` | Exposes the source-shaped theorem surface for Problem 2.22.  The first theorem packages the guard-digit/Sterbenz exactness step: under ordered-side hypotheses, the finite round-to-even `a-b` step in Kahan's parenthesized Heron formula is exact.  The second theorem re-exports the full finite trace bound from `Heron.lean`: under the listed finite-normal range hypotheses for the remaining rounded intermediates and `gammaValid` side condition, the computed area has relative error at most `(1 + gamma_9) * (1 + u)^2 - 1` relative to the exact Kahan expression.  Full IEEE special-value/flag semantics and a theorem eliminating the explicit range hypotheses remain open. |
| Higham Chapter 2 Problem 2.23 guard-digit versus no-guard sequence | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/Problem2_23.lean` | `problem2_23_guardDigitY`, `problem2_23_noGuardY`, `problem2_23_binaryNoGuardScaledMantissa`, `problem2_23_binaryScaledValue`, `problem2_23_binaryNoGuardYScaled` | `problem2_23_guardDigitY_eq_x_of_finiteSystem`, `problem2_23_noGuardY_error_formula`, `problem2_23_binaryNoGuardScaledMantissa_eq_add_mod_two`, `problem2_23_binaryNoGuardScaledMantissa_eq_self_of_even`, `problem2_23_binaryNoGuardScaledMantissa_eq_succ_of_odd`, `problem2_23_binaryNoGuardYScaled_error_eq_low_bit`, `problem2_23_binaryNoGuardYScaled_eq_scaledValue_add_low_bit`, `problem2_23_guard_and_binary_noGuard_summary` | Formalizes the source computation `y = (x+x)-x` at two levels.  On the finite exact/guard-digit path, if `x` and `x+x` remain in the finite system, the rounded sequence returns `x`.  In the abstract no-guard model, Lean exposes the full perturbation formula `y-x = x*(alpha+beta+2*gamma+alpha*gamma+beta*gamma-eta)`.  At the binary mantissa level, dropping the guard bit changes the scaled result from mantissa `m` to `m + m % 2`, equivalently adding `(m % 2) * scale` to the original scaled value; the packaged theorem states the guard and no-guard conclusions together.  Full executable binary hardware semantics, overflow/underflow, and IEEE special-value behavior remain open. |

round-to-even path for `(((x - 0.5) + x) - 0.5) + x` and exposes the rounded intermediates.  The exact source expression is `3*x - 1`, is zero exactly at `x = 1/3`, and is nonzero away from `1/3`; Lean also proves that `0.5` is finite in the modeled IEEE single and double formats and that finite IEEE single/double inputs are not `1/3`.  The exact-intermediate branch proves the rounded path equals `3*x - 1` when all exact real intermediates are finite.  The supplemental zero-result rows now show that any modeled finite IEEE zero branch would force the third exact real intermediate to be both nonfinite and finite representable on the localized `[3/10,3/8]` interval.  Therefore `problem2_24_ieeeSingle_eval_ne_zero_of_finiteSystem_input` and `problem2_24_ieeeDouble_eval_ne_zero_of_finiteSystem_input` close the finite single/double round-to-even model.  Remaining Problem 2.24 work is the lift to full IEEE flags, traps, infinities, and NaNs. |
| Higham Chapter 2 Problem 2.25 Kahan FMA determinant exact-residual core | `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/Problem2_25.lean`, `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/FusedMultiplyAdd.lean`, `LeanFpAnalysis/FP/Analysis/CramersRule.lean` | `problem2_25_fmaCorrection`, `problem2_25_fmaMain`, `problem2_25_fmaCore`, `problem2_25_finiteFmaCorrection`, `problem2_25_finiteFmaMain`, `problem2_25_finiteFmaCore`, `problem2_25_finiteFmaDet`, `problem2_25_finiteRoundedProduct`, `problem2_25_finiteFmaDetWithRoundedProduct`, `problem2_25_roundedProductResidualsRepresentable` | `problem2_25_fmaCore_eq_det2x2`, `problem2_25_finiteFmaCorrection_eq_exact_of_finiteSystem`, `problem2_25_finiteFmaMain_eq_exact_of_finiteSystem`, `problem2_25_finiteFmaCore_eq_det2x2_of_exact_residuals`, `problem2_25_finiteFmaDet_signedRelErrorWitness_lt`, `problem2_25_finiteFmaDet_relError_lt_unitRoundoff`, `problem2_25_finiteRoundedProduct_finiteSystem`, `problem2_25_finiteFmaDetWithRoundedProduct_signedRelErrorWitness_lt`, `problem2_25_finiteFmaDetWithRoundedProduct_relError_lt_unitRoundoff`, `problem2_25_finiteFmaDetWithRoundedProduct_highRelativeAccuracy` | Formalizes the exact-residual theorem surface for Kahan's FMA determinant algorithm.  For `det [[a,b],[c,d]] = a*d - b*c`, the exact FMA core `(a*d-w) + (w-b*c)` equals the determinant for any rounded product placeholder `w`.  If the two FMA residuals are finite-system representable, the finite round-to-even FMA core is exactly the determinant; if the determinant is also in finite-normal range, the final rounded result has relative error strictly below `unitRoundoff`.  The source-shaped wrapper now uses the displayed first computation `w = fl(b*c)`, proves that rounded product is finite, names the exact-residual side condition for this `w`, and packages the signed-witness plus relative-error theorem for the displayed algorithm.  The binary/IEEE proof that the residual representability hypotheses hold for the intended input class and the special-value/flag semantics remain open. |
